title: How to Trust Your Gut When Your Mind Disagrees
meta_description: Learn to trust your gut even when your mind fights back. Practical steps to hear your inner knowing and make decisions with clarity and confidence.
og_title: How to Trust Your Gut When Your Mind Disagrees
og_description: Learn to trust your gut even when your mind fights back. Practical steps to hear your inner knowing and make decisions with clarity and confidence.
body:
How to Trust Your Gut When Your Mind Disagrees

You know that feeling. You're standing at a crossroads - maybe it's a job offer, a relationship decision, or just whether to go to that party tonight. Your mind is running through pros and cons, making lists, analyzing every angle. And then there's that quiet pull in your chest. Something that says "yes" when your brain says "no," or "no" when everything looks perfect on paper.

Here's the thing nobody tells you: that pull? It's not random. It's not wishful thinking. And it's definitely not your anxiety acting up - though your mind will try to convince you it is. That pull is your gut, and it's been trying to talk to you your whole life. You just haven't been taught how to listen.

I'm Kalesh, and I work with people who feel things deeply. People who walk into rooms and know the mood before anyone speaks. People who get "wrong feelings" about people who seem perfectly nice. People who've been told they're "too sensitive" or "overthinking things" when really, they're just picking up on stuff most people miss. If that's you, you're not broken. Your nervous system is just calibrated for a wider range than most. And learning to trust your gut when your mind disagrees? That's not about choosing feelings over logic. It's about learning which voice is actually telling the truth.

"Your gut isn't the opposite of your mind - it's the part of you that sees the whole picture while your mind is still squinting at the details."

Why Your Mind Lies to You (Even When It Means Well)

Let's get one thing straight: your mind isn't trying to sabotage you. Your rational brain has kept you alive this long. It remembers that time you touched a hot stove, and it's good at spotting patterns that look like danger. But here's the problem - your mind is also really good at making up stories based on incomplete information. Right?!

Think about it. How many times have you talked yourself out of something that felt right because your brain came up with a dozen reasons why it wouldn't work? "I don't have enough experience." "What if I fail?" "What will people think?" Those aren't facts. Those are your mind's fear-based projections, dressed up to look like logic.

Your gut, on the other hand, doesn't do projections. It doesn't care about what other people think. It doesn't run cost-benefit analyses based on past trauma. Your gut responds to the present moment - the energy of a room, the tone of someone's voice, the subtle shift in your body when something's off. It's not smarter than your mind. But it's more honest.

Does that land? Good. Because here's where most people get stuck - they think trusting their gut means abandoning their brain. It doesn't. It means giving your gut veto power when your brain is stuck in a loop.

The Signal vs. The Noise: How to Tell the Difference

One of the biggest questions I get is: "How do I know it's my gut and not my anxiety?" Fair question. Anxiety feels like a fire alarm going off in your chest. Your gut feels like a quiet tug. Anxiety is loud, urgent, and repetitive. Your gut is calm, clear, and usually says something once.

Here's a simple way to check: when you're trying to decide something, pay attention to where the feeling lives in your body. Anxiety tends to sit in your head - racing thoughts, tension in your jaw, shallow breathing. Your gut lives lower - in your stomach, your chest, your solar plexus. It's a sense of expansion or contraction. When something's right for you, your body opens up. When something's wrong, you close down. It's that simple.

I had a client once who was trying to decide whether to take a promotion at work. The offer was amazing - more money, more prestige, everything she'd been working toward. But every time she thought about saying yes, her stomach would knot up. Her mind told her she was being ungrateful. Her gut said something else entirely. She took the job anyway, because the logic was too good to ignore. Six months later, she was miserable. The promotion came with a toxic boss and a culture that drained her. Her gut knew from day one. She just didn't listen.

Don't let that be you. Your gut doesn't need a spreadsheet to know when something's off. It just knows. And the more you ignore it, the quieter it gets. Until one day, you can't hear it at all.

Why "Following Your Gut" Feels So Risky

Let's be real for a second. Trusting your gut feels terrifying because it's not provable. You can't show someone a gut feeling and say, "See? This is why I'm making this choice." You can't defend it in a board meeting or explain it to your skeptical friend. It's just... a knowing. And that kind of knowing doesn't come with a receipt.

But here's what I've learned from working with hundreds of sensitive, intuitive people: the things you regret most aren't the times you trusted your gut and got it wrong. They're the times you ignored it and got it right - and then spent years wondering what would've happened if you'd listened. Know what I mean?

There's a reason that phrase "I had a gut feeling" exists in every language and culture. It's not a metaphor. It's a biological reality. Your gut has its own nervous system - scientists call it the "second brain." It's got about 100 million neurons, and it's constantly sending signals to your brain that you can learn to read. The problem isn't that your gut is unreliable. The problem is that you've been trained to override it.

How to Start Trusting Your Gut Today (Yes, Today)

You don't need to meditate for five years or become a monk to start trusting your gut. You just need to practice paying attention. Here are three things you can do right now:

1. Start with Low-Stakes Decisions

Don't start with "Should I quit my job and move to another country?" Start with what to eat for lunch. What route to take on your walk. Which shirt to wear. Before you make a choice, pause. Breathe. Ask your body: "What feels right?" Then notice what happens. You might feel a slight relaxation in your shoulders when you think about the chicken salad instead of the sandwich. That's your gut. Practice noticing it on the small stuff, and you'll get better at hearing it on the big stuff.

2. Get Out of Your Head and Into Your Body

Your gut doesn't speak in words. It speaks in sensations. So if you're trying to make a decision and your mind is going a million miles an hour, stop. Put your hand on your belly. Take three slow breaths. Then ask the question again. Notice what shifts. Does your chest feel tight? Is there a sinking feeling in your stomach? Or does everything soften and open? That physical response is your answer. Your mind will try to argue with it. Let it. But trust the body first.

3. Keep a Gut Journal

For the next week, write down every time you have a gut feeling - big or small. What did it feel like? Did you listen or ignore it? What happened? Over time, you'll start to see patterns. You'll notice that your gut is right way more often than you think. And you'll start to build evidence for yourself that this knowing is real. If you want a structured way to do this, I recommend picking up a journal that helps you track these signals. See on Amazon (paid link) - this one has prompts that make it easy to start.

What to Do When Your Gut and Your Mind Are at War

This is the hard part. You've got your gut saying one thing and your mind screaming the opposite. How do you decide? Here's my honest answer: you don't have to choose one over the other. You just have to figure out which one is telling the truth about this particular moment.

Your mind is great at logistics. It can tell you if you have enough money, if the timing makes sense, if the risks are manageable. Your gut is great at alignment. It can tell you if something is right for your soul, even if it looks wrong on paper. The key is to let them do their separate jobs. Let your mind handle the practical details. Let your gut handle the deeper knowing.

So when they disagree, ask yourself: "Is my mind worried about something real, or is it spinning a story based on fear?" If it's a real practical concern - like not having enough savings to make a move - then you can address that. But if your mind is saying "you're not good enough" or "what if everyone judges you"? That's not logic. That's fear dressed up as logic. And your gut is the only one that can see through it.

I've been through this myself more times than I can count. There was a time when I was offered a teaching position at a well-known school. It was stable, respected, and paid well. My mind said yes. My gut said no. I went back and forth for weeks. Finally, I sat down and asked myself: "If I take this job, what am I giving up?" The answer wasn't about money or prestige. It was about freedom. My gut knew that the structure would suffocate me, even though my mind saw it as security. I turned it down. It was terrifying. And it was the right call.

The Bright Wound: Why Sensitivity Is Your Superpower

Here's the part that might sting a little. You've probably been told your whole life that you feel too much. That you're too sensitive. That you need to toughen up. And maybe you've internalized that. Maybe you've learned to ignore your gut because every time you followed it, someone told you you were wrong.

But here's the truth: that sensitivity isn't a weakness. It's a sign that your intuition is awake. You're not feeling "too much" - you're feeling what everyone else is ignoring. That's why they call it "the bright wound." The same sensitivity that makes you feel overwhelmed by crowds and drained by certain people is the same sensitivity that lets you read a room, know who to trust, and feel the truth before it's spoken.

Your gut is the part of you that survived because it could sense danger before it arrived. It's ancient. It's wise. And it's been trying to protect you your whole life. The only reason you don't trust it is because you've been taught to value logic over feeling. But logic is just a tool. Your gut is a compass.

If you want to go deeper into this, I highly recommend checking out See on Amazon (paid link) - it's one of the best books I've found on understanding how your sensitivity and intuition are connected. It'll change how you see yourself.

How to Make the Leap When You're Scared

Okay, so you've started listening to your gut. You've noticed the signals. But now you're at a real decision point, and your gut is saying something that terrifies you. Maybe it's leaving a relationship. Maybe it's starting a business. Maybe it's saying no to something everyone expects you to say yes to.

Here's the thing: courage isn't the absence of fear. It's feeling the fear and doing it anyway. Your gut doesn't ask you to be fearless. It asks you to be honest. And sometimes being honest means making a choice that doesn't make sense to anyone but you.

One thing that helps is to ask yourself: "If I knew I couldn't fail, what would I choose?" That's not about delusion. It's about stripping away the fear-based noise and getting to what you actually want. Your gut answers that question instantly. Your mind takes forever because it's trying to figure out how to make it work. Let your gut answer first. Then let your mind figure out the logistics.

Another tool: imagine yourself five years from now, looking back at this moment. What decision would you regret more - following your gut and being wrong, or ignoring it and always wondering? For most people, the answer is clear. The regret of not trying is heavier than the regret of trying and failing.

Practical Exercises to Strengthen Your Gut Instinct

I'm going to give you three exercises you can do this week. They're simple. They're not spiritual or woo-woo. They're just practice.

Exercise 1: The Two-Door Test

When you're facing a decision, imagine two doors. Behind one door is Option A. Behind the other is Option B. Don't think. Just notice which door your body leans toward. It might be subtle - a slight tilt forward, a relaxation in your chest. That's your gut voting. Trust it.

Exercise 2: The 10-Second Rule

When someone asks you a question or presents an opportunity, don't answer immediately. Take 10 seconds. Breathe. Notice what comes up before your mind starts editing. That first impulse? That's your gut. Your mind will try to change it. Write down the first thing you felt before you talked yourself out of it.

Exercise 3: The Body Scan Decision

Sit quietly. Bring the decision to mind. Notice where you feel it in your body. Is there tightness? Expansion? A pull? A push? Stay with it for a full minute. Don't try to change it. Just observe. Your body knows the answer. You just have to be still enough to hear it.

If you want a more structured approach to building this skill, See on Amazon (paid link) has some excellent exercises that walk you through this step by step. It's a great companion for anyone serious about developing their intuition.

The Final Word: You Already Know

Here's the thing I want you to walk away with. You already know how to trust your gut. You've done it before. You've had those moments where you just knew something was right or wrong, and you acted on it, and it worked out. The problem isn't that you can't hear your gut. The problem is that you've been taught to doubt it.

Every time you second-guess yourself, every time you ask someone else what you should do, every time you override that quiet knowing with loud logic - you're training yourself to ignore your deepest wisdom. And that's a tragedy. Because your gut is the most honest part of you. It doesn't lie. It doesn't manipulate. It doesn't care about looking good or being right. It just knows.

So here's your invitation: start small. Listen to your gut on the little things today. What do you actually want for dinner? What route feels better? Which person do you feel drawn to call? Pay attention. Notice what it feels like when you follow that pull. And when you get it right - and you will - celebrate it. That's how you build trust. Not by being perfect. But by showing up, over and over, and choosing to listen.

Your gut has been waiting for you to hear it. It's been patient. It's been quiet. But it's still there. And it's still telling you the truth. All you have to do is stop arguing and start listening.

If you're ready to go deeper and start truly trusting your inner guidance, I've put together a short assessment that can help you figure out where you're getting stuck. Take the free intuition quiz here and get clarity on what's blocking your gut from speaking clearly.
